title: Shanghai's Dongfang Suanxin unveils DF1000, a 14nm HBM-free AI chip that skips the parts the U.S. controls
subtitle: The startup — backed by state funds, Meituan, Xiaomi, and JD.com — claims 520 BF16 teraflops and 6.4 TB/s of memory bandwidth from a 3D-stacked near-memory design on a mature process node.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Dongfang Suanxin — English-name Orient Silicon, based in Shanghai's Zhangjiang district — unveiled its DF1000 AI accelerator on July 13, ahead of the World Artificial Intelligence Conference. The company claims 520 teraflops of BF16 compute, 6.4 terabytes per second of memory bandwidth, and 900 gigabytes per second of scale-up interconnect. The unusual part is what the chip does not use: it is built on a mature 14-nanometer process, it does not use HBM, and it relies instead on a 3D-stacked near-memory architecture with what the company calls 'software-defined computing.' Mass production is planned for end-of-year 2026, with the DF2000 targeted for Q4 to double performance and, the company claims, surpass Nvidia's H200. Backers include state funds, Meituan, Xiaomi, and JD.com at a RMB 12.3 billion valuation.

The design choice is the whole story. U.S. export controls hit two specific chokepoints: leading-edge lithography — which caps China at roughly 7nm — and HBM3/HBM3E memory. A conventional AI accelerator design needs both, so a Chinese startup building one on 14nm without HBM looks impossible on paper. Dongfang Suanxin is arguing you can substitute architecture for process technology: stack cheaper memory dies vertically close to compute, tune the software stack around the constraint, and get to competitive bandwidth without touching the parts Washington controls. Whether the claimed numbers survive third-party testing is the open question, but the strategic move is legible.

This fits a pattern getting clearer week by week. Alibaba banning Claude Code and pushing developers onto its own Qoder platform. Meituan shipping LongCat 2 on frontier Chinese chips. DeepSeek building an in-house inference chip to escape Nvidia dependence. The Ministry of Commerce drafting export controls on Chinese AI models. Zhipu's founder publicly arguing frontier AI should stay open. The through-line is that China is treating the AI stack — hardware, models, developer tools — as a national industrial system to be built domestically, and export controls are accelerating that decoupling rather than slowing it.

A note for learners: the phrase 'AI compute' hides a stack that is easy to caricature and hard to actually understand. What Nvidia sells is not just a chip — it is a chip, HBM memory sourced from Korea, a networking fabric, a driver stack, and CUDA. Every one of those layers is a potential chokepoint under export controls, and every one is where a Chinese competitor might substitute a domestic option. If you want to reason about the AI supply chain in 2026, learning what HBM is, why it matters, and what 3D stacking substitutes for it is a better use of an afternoon than reading yet another model-benchmark tweet.
